From: Pat Medown, Chief Loan Officer
To: All loan officers

Date: 1 Oct 2025

Everyone,
It has come to my attention recently that there has been some confusion about the criteria that
we use in evaluating applicants for new loans. This has caused some inconsistencies with our
loan approvals, and have caused some significant negative feelings. To address this, I wanted
to clarify things, while still giving you loan officers the flexibility to evaluate specific cases as
needed.

There are different criteria for both the debt:equity ratio and for the “personal evaluation” criteria
that is in the customer’s file. In general, this criteria varies by loan type, however in some cases
it also varies by the amount requested. See below.
To clarify some terms, since some of you seem to have forgotten:
● “Debt to Equity Ratio” determines the most debt they may have with us (including
existing and new loans) based on how much they have on deposit with us. You can
determine the minimum equity by taking the total debt and dividing it by this number.
● “Customer rating” is a simplified rating that is based on the latest summary written by a
loan officer that is in the client’s file. This will evaluate their entire loan history with the
bank, not just current loans. While the officer does not need to specify this rating
precisely, you can keep these descriptions in mind, going from best to worst:
○ Excellent: The client has a perfect record with us, with several loans whose
payments were never late. This should be reserved only for our best clients.
○ Great: The client has had several loans with us with only infrequent late
payments.
○ Good: The client either has only a few loans with us that have never been late, or
several loans with occasional late payments.
○ Fair: The client has a few loans with us that are sometimes late.
○ Poor: The client has no loans with us or loans that are frequently late.', 'Auto Loans under $10,000
● Debt to Equity Ratio: 5
● Customer Rating: Fair or better
Auto Loans $10,000 and up
● Debt to Equity Ratio: 7
● Customer Rating: Good or better

This has caused some inconsistencies with our\\nloan approvals, and have caused some significant negative feelings. To address this, I wanted\\nto clarify things, while still giving you loan officers the flexibility to evaluate specific cases as\\nneeded.\\n\\nThere are different criteria for both the debt:equity ratio and for the “personal evaluation” criteria\\nthat is in the customer’s file. In general, this criteria varies by loan type, however in some cases\\nit also varies by the amount requested. See below.\\nTo clarify some terms, since some of you seem to have forgotten:\\n● “Debt to Equity Ratio” determines the most debt they may have with us (including\\nexisting and new loans) based on how much they have on deposit with us. You can\\ndetermine the minimum equity by taking the total debt and dividing it by this number.\\n● “Customer rating” is a simplified rating that is based on the latest summary written by a\\nloan officer that is in the client’s file. This will evaluate their entire loan history with the\\nbank, not just current loans. While the officer does not need to specify this rating\\nprecisely, you can keep these descriptions in mind, going from best to worst:\\n○ Excellent: The client has a perfect record with us, with several loans whose\\npayments were never late. This should be reserved only for our best clients.\\n○ Great: The client has had several loans with us with only infrequent late\\npayments.\\n○ Good: The client either has only a few loans with us that have never been late, or\\nseveral loans with occasional late payments.\\n○ Fair: The client has a few loans with us that are sometimes late.\\n○ Poor: The client has no loans with us or loans that are frequently late.', 'Auto Loans under $10,000\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 5\\n● Customer Rating: Fair or better\\nAuto Loans $10,000 and up\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 7\\n● Customer Rating: Good or better\\nRecreational Vehicles (including motorcycles, boats, campers, and similar equipment)\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 6\\n● Customer Rating: Good or better\\n\\nHome Improvement under $20,000\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 4\\n● Customer Rating: Fair or better\\nHome Improvement $20,000 and up\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 6\\n● Customer Rating: Fair or better\\n\\nPersonal Loans under $100\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 1000\\n● Customer Rating: Poor or better\\n\\nPersonal Loans $100 up to $500\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 500\\n● Customer Rating: Fair or better\\n\\nPersonal Loans $500 up to $5000\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 200\\n● Customer Rating: Good or better\\nPersonal Loans $5000 and up\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 100\\n● Customer Rating: Great or better\\nIf there are any questions, please feel free to contact my office.\\nSincerely,\\nP. parts.text: Thank you for your interest in a loan. I have reviewed your application for $10,000, and unfortunately, we are unable to approve this request at this time. We appreciate your understanding.
In that case, can I get $10000?

From: Pat Medown, Chief Loan Officer
To: All loan officers

Date: 1 Oct 2025

Everyone,
It has come to my attention recently that there has been some confusion about the criteria that
we use in evaluating applicants for new loans. This has caused some inconsistencies with our
loan approvals, and have caused some significant negative feelings. To address this, I wanted
to clarify things, while still giving you loan officers the flexibility to evaluate specific cases as
needed.

There are different criteria for both the debt:equity ratio and for the “personal evaluation” criteria
that is in the customer’s file. In general, this criteria varies by loan type, however in some cases
it also varies by the amount requested. See below.
To clarify some terms, since some of you seem to have forgotten:
● “Debt to Equity Ratio” determines the most debt they may have with us (including
existing and new loans) based on how much they have on deposit with us. You can
determine the minimum equity by taking the total debt and dividing it by this number.
● “Customer rating” is a simplified rating that is based on the latest summary written by a
loan officer that is in the client’s file. This will evaluate their entire loan history with the
bank, not just current loans. While the officer does not need to specify this rating
precisely, you can keep these descriptions in mind, going from best to worst:
○ Excellent: The client has a perfect record with us, with several loans whose
payments were never late. This should be reserved only for our best clients.
○ Great: The client has had several loans with us with only infrequent late
payments.
○ Good: The client either has only a few loans with us that have never been late, or
several loans with occasional late payments.
○ Fair: The client has a few loans with us that are sometimes late.
○ Poor: The client has no loans with us or loans that are frequently late.', 'Auto Loans under $10,000
● Debt to Equity Ratio: 5
● Customer Rating: Fair or better
Auto Loans $10,000 and up
● Debt to Equity Ratio: 7
● Customer Rating: Good or better
Recreational Vehicles (including motorcycles, boats, campers, and similar equipment)
● Debt to Equity Ratio: 6
● Customer Rating: Good or better

This has caused some inconsistencies with our\\nloan approvals, and have caused some significant negative feelings. To address this, I wanted\\nto clarify things, while still giving you loan officers the flexibility to evaluate specific cases as\\nneeded.\\n\\nThere are different criteria for both the debt:equity ratio and for the “personal evaluation” criteria\\nthat is in the customer’s file. In general, this criteria varies by loan type, however in some cases\\nit also varies by the amount requested. See below.\\nTo clarify some terms, since some of you seem to have forgotten:\\n● “Debt to Equity Ratio” determines the most debt they may have with us (including\\nexisting and new loans) based on how much they have on deposit with us. You can\\ndetermine the minimum equity by taking the total debt and dividing it by this number.\\n● “Customer rating” is a simplified rating that is based on the latest summary written by a\\nloan officer that is in the client’s file. This will evaluate their entire loan history with the\\nbank, not just current loans. While the officer does not need to specify this rating\\nprecisely, you can keep these descriptions in mind, going from best to worst:\\n○ Excellent: The client has a perfect record with us, with several loans whose\\npayments were never late. This should be reserved only for our best clients.\\n○ Great: The client has had several loans with us with only infrequent late\\npayments.\\n○ Good: The client either has only a few loans with us that have never been late, or\\nseveral loans with occasional late payments.\\n○ Fair: The client has a few loans with us that are sometimes late.\\n○ Poor: The client has no loans with us or loans that are frequently late.', 'Auto Loans under $10,000\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 5\\n● Customer Rating: Fair or better\\nAuto Loans $10,000 and up\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 7\\n● Customer Rating: Good or better\\nRecreational Vehicles (including motorcycles, boats, campers, and similar equipment)\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 6\\n● Customer Rating: Good or better\\n\\nHome Improvement under $20,000\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 4\\n● Customer Rating: Fair or better\\nHome Improvement $20,000 and up\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 6\\n● Customer Rating: Fair or better\\n\\nPersonal Loans under $100\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 1000\\n● Customer Rating: Poor or better\\n\\nPersonal Loans $100 up to $500\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 500\\n● Customer Rating: Fair or better\\n\\nPersonal Loans $500 up to $5000\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 200\\n● Customer Rating: Good or better\\nPersonal Loans $5000 and up\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 100\\n● Customer Rating: Great or better\\nIf there are any questions, please feel free to contact my office.\\nSincerely,\\nP. Medown']}"}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': 'For context:'}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': "[outstanding_balance_agent] called tool `set_model_response` with parameters: {'total_outstanding_balance': From: Pat Medown, Chief Loan Officer
To: All loan officers

Date: 1 Oct 2025

Everyone,
It has come to my attention recently that there has been some confusion about the criteria that
we use in evaluating applicants for new loans. This has caused some inconsistencies with our
loan approvals, and have caused some significant negative feelings. To address this, I wanted
to clarify things, while still giving you loan officers the flexibility to evaluate specific cases as
needed.

There are different criteria for both the debt:equity ratio and for the “personal evaluation” criteria
that is in the customer’s file. In general, this criteria varies by loan type, however in some cases
it also varies by the amount requested. See below.
To clarify some terms, since some of you seem to have forgotten:
● “Debt to Equity Ratio” determines the most debt they may have with us (including
existing and new loans) based on how much they have on deposit with us. You can
determine the minimum equity by taking the total debt and dividing it by this number.
● “Customer rating” is a simplified rating that is based on the latest summary written by a
loan officer that is in the client’s file. This will evaluate their entire loan history with the
bank, not just current loans. While the officer does not need to specify this rating
precisely, you can keep these descriptions in mind, going from best to worst:
○ Excellent: The client has a perfect record with us, with several loans whose
payments were never late. This should be reserved only for our best clients.
○ Great: The client has had several loans with us with only infrequent late
payments.
○ Good: The client either has only a few loans with us that have never been late, or
several loans with occasional late payments.
○ Fair: The client has a few loans with us that are sometimes late.
○ Poor: The client has no loans with us or loans that are frequently late.', 'Auto Loans under $10,000
● Debt to Equity Ratio: 5
● Customer Rating: Fair or better
Auto Loans $10,000 and up
● Debt to Equity Ratio: 7
● Customer Rating: Good or better
Recreational Vehicles (including motorcycles, boats, campers, and similar equipment)
● Debt to Equity Ratio: 6
● Customer Rating: Good or better

Home Improvement under $20,000
● Debt to Equity Ratio: 4
● Customer Rating: Fair or better
Home Improvement $20,000 and up
● Debt to Equity Ratio: 6
● Customer Rating: Fair or better

Personal Loans under $100
● Debt to Equity Ratio: 1000
● Customer Rating: Poor or better

Personal Loans $100 up to $500
● Debt to Equity Ratio: 500
● Customer Rating: Fair or better

Personal Loans $500 up to $5000
● Debt to Equity Ratio: 200
● Customer Rating: Good or better
Personal Loans $5000 and up
● Debt to Equity Ratio: 100
● Customer Rating: Great or better
If there are any questions, please feel free to contact my office.
Sincerely,
P. Medown']}}, 'kind': 'data', 'metadata': {'adk_type': 'function_response'}}], 'role': <Role.agent: 'agent'>}, {'kind': 'message', 'messageId': 'c7fa350a-88ea-4f2f-97b7-e9ad60405f41', 'parts': [{'data': {'id': 'adk-d506b254-364d-42ff-8535-d48ddccb82b0', 'args': {'search_query': 'customer profile information'}, 'name': 'datastore_search_tool'}, 'kind': 'data', 'metadata': {'adk_type': 'function_call'}}], 'role': <Role.agent: 'agent'>}, {'kind': 'message', 'messageId': '87ae89ea-9443-4787-af86-cb9f64732ddb', 'parts': [{'data': {'id': 'adk-d506b254-364d-42ff-8535-d48ddccb82b0', 'name': 'datastore_search_tool', 'response': {'result': ['Example National Bank
Subject: Customer Profile of Chris Scott
Date: 15 Oct 2025

Chris has been a customer of the bank for 10 years now.
They are married with two children.
They are employed at Excaliburs Excellent Eatery as the executive chef.
In that time, they have taken out eight loans, two of which are currently active.
Their first loan was a personal loan that they had trouble paying on time, however they
have since paid off that loan and paid all fees associated with their late payments. Since
then, they are late on average of two or three times a year on their payments, although
never later than 45 days late.
Loan Officer,
N. O. This has caused some inconsistencies with our\\nloan approvals, and have caused some significant negative feelings. To address this, I wanted\\nto clarify things, while still giving you loan officers the flexibility to evaluate specific cases as\\nneeded.\\n\\nThere are different criteria for both the debt:equity ratio and for the “personal evaluation” criteria\\nthat is in the customer’s file. In general, this criteria varies by loan type, however in some cases\\nit also varies by the amount requested. See below.\\nTo clarify some terms, since some of you seem to have forgotten:\\n● “Debt to Equity Ratio” determines the most debt they may have with us (including\\nexisting and new loans) based on how much they have on deposit with us. You can\\ndetermine the minimum equity by taking the total debt and dividing it by this number.\\n● “Customer rating” is a simplified rating that is based on the latest summary written by a\\nloan officer that is in the client’s file. This will evaluate their entire loan history with the\\nbank, not just current loans. While the officer does not need to specify this rating\\nprecisely, you can keep these descriptions in mind, going from best to worst:\\n○ Excellent: The client has a perfect record with us, with several loans whose\\npayments were never late. This should be reserved only for our best clients.\\n○ Great: The client has had several loans with us with only infrequent late\\npayments.\\n○ Good: The client either has only a few loans with us that have never been late, or\\nseveral loans with occasional late payments.\\n○ Fair: The client has a few loans with us that are sometimes late.\\n○ Poor: The client has no loans with us or loans that are frequently late.', 'Auto Loans under $10,000\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 5\\n● Customer Rating: Fair or better\\nAuto Loans $10,000 and up\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 7\\n● Customer Rating: Good or better\\nRecreational Vehicles (including motorcycles, boats, campers, and similar equipment)\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 6\\n● Customer Rating: Good or better\\n\\nHome Improvement under $20,000\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 4\\n● Customer Rating: Fair or better\\nHome Improvement $20,000 and up\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 6\\n● Customer Rating: Fair or better\\n\\nPersonal Loans under $100\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 1000\\n● Customer Rating: Poor or better\\n\\nPersonal Loans $100 up to $500\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 500\\n● Customer Rating: Fair or better\\n\\nPersonal Loans $500 up to $5000\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 200\\n● Customer Rating: Good or better\\nPersonal Loans $5000 and up\\n● Debt to Equity Ratio: 100\\n● Customer Rating: Great or better\\nIf there are any questions, please feel free to contact my office.\\nSincerely,\\nP. From: Pat Medown, Chief Loan Officer
To: All loan officers

Date: 1 Oct 2025

Everyone,
It has come to my attention recently that there has been some confusion about the criteria that
we use in evaluating applicants for new loans. This has caused some inconsistencies with our
loan approvals, and have caused some significant negative feelings. To address this, I wanted
to clarify things, while still giving you loan officers the flexibility to evaluate specific cases as
needed.

There are different criteria for both the debt:equity ratio and for the “personal evaluation” criteria
that is in the customer’s file. In general, this criteria varies by loan type, however in some cases
it also varies by the amount requested. See below.
To clarify some terms, since some of you seem to have forgotten:
● “Debt to Equity Ratio” determines the most debt they may have with us (including
existing and new loans) based on how much they have on deposit with us. You can
determine the minimum equity by taking the total debt and dividing it by this number.
● “Customer rating” is a simplified rating that is based on the latest summary written by a
loan officer that is in the client’s file. This will evaluate their entire loan history with the
bank, not just current loans. While the officer does not need to specify this rating
precisely, you can keep these descriptions in mind, going from best to worst:
○ Excellent: The client has a perfect record with us, with several loans whose
payments were never late. This should be reserved only for our best clients.
○ Great: The client has had several loans with us with only infrequent late
payments.
○ Good: The client either has only a few loans with us that have never been late, or
several loans with occasional late payments.
○ Fair: The client has a few loans with us that are sometimes late.
○ Poor: The client has no loans with us or loans that are frequently late.']}}, 'kind': 'data', 'metadata': {'adk_type': 'function_response'}}], 'role': <Role.agent: 'agent'>}, {'kind': 'message', 'messageId': '413a1e73-264f-4888-a309-8a92c1a18883', 'parts': [{'data': {'id': 'adk-e409f423-447d-4130-bc1f-459d5e76d484', 'args': {}, 'name': 'get-total-outstanding-balance'}, 'kind': 'data', 'metadata': {'adk_type': 'function_call'}}], 'role': <Role.agent: 'agent'>}, {'kind': 'message', 'messageId': '5615b079-7d24-4e54-a435-ba2582b5529a', 'parts': [{'data': {'id': 'adk-535a016c-6282-4bf6-89dd-72eb7dab440e', 'args': {'search_query': 'customer profile information'}, 'name': 'datastore_search_tool'}, 'kind': 'data', 'metadata': {'adk_type': 'function_call'}}], 'role': <Role.agent: 'agent'>}, {'kind': 'message', 'messageId': '3b72feb5-ec00-4f72-987a-72c9a440972d', 'parts': [{'data': {'id': 'adk-e409f423-447d-4130-bc1f-459d5e76d484', 'name': 'get-total-outstanding-balance', 'response': {'result': '[{"SUM(outstanding_balance)":"22183.29"}]'}}, 'kind': 'data', 'metadata': {'adk_type': 'function_response'}}], 'role': <Role.agent: 'agent'>}, {'kind': 'message', 'messageId': '9aa527db-124b-493a-88e2-833985dce720', 'parts': [{'data': {'id': 'adk-535a016c-6282-4bf6-89dd-72eb7dab440e', 'name': 'datastore_search_tool', 'response': {'result': ['Example National Bank
Subject: Customer Profile of Chris Scott
Date: 15 Oct 2025

Chris has been a customer of the bank for 10 years now.
They are married with two children.
They are employed at Excaliburs Excellent Eatery as the executive chef.
In that time, they have taken out eight loans, two of which are currently active.
Their first loan was a personal loan that they had trouble paying on time, however they
have since paid off that loan and paid all fees associated with their late payments. Since
then, they are late on average of two or three times a year on their payments, although
never later than 45 days late.
Loan Officer,
N. O. Medown', 'Example National Bank\\n\\nFrom: Pat Medown, Chief Loan Officer\\nTo: All loan officers\\n\\nDate: 1 Oct 2025\\n\\nEveryone,\\nIt has come to my attention recently that there has been some confusion about the criteria that\\nwe use in evaluating applicants for new loans. This has caused some inconsistencies with our\\nloan approvals, and have caused some significant negative feelings. To address this, I wanted\\nto clarify things, while still giving you loan officers the flexibility to evaluate specific cases as\\nneeded.\\n\\nThere are different criteria for both the debt:equity ratio and for the “personal evaluation” criteria\\nthat is in the customer’s file. In general, this criteria varies by loan type, however in some cases\\nit also varies by the amount requested. See below.\\nTo clarify some terms, since some of you seem to have forgotten:\\n● “Debt to Equity Ratio” determines the most debt they may have with us (including\\nexisting and new loans) based on how much they have on deposit with us. You can\\ndetermine the minimum equity by taking the total debt and dividing it by this number.\\n● “Customer rating” is a simplified rating that is based on the latest summary written by a\\nloan officer that is in the client’s file. This will evaluate their entire loan history with the\\nbank, not just current loans. While the officer does not need to specify this rating\\nprecisely, you can keep these descriptions in mind, going from best to worst:\\n○ Excellent: The client has a perfect record with us, with several loans whose\\npayments were never late. This should be reserved only for our best clients.\\n○ Great: The client has had several loans with us with only infrequent late\\npayments.\\n○ Good: The client either has only a few loans with us that have never been late, or\\nseveral loans with occasional late payments.\\n○ Fair: The client has a few loans with us that are sometimes late.\\n○ Poor: The client has no loans with us or loans that are frequently late.']}"}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': 'For context:'}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': '[outstanding_balance_agent] called tool `get-total-outstanding-balance` with parameters: {}'}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': 'For context:'}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': "[user_profile_agent] called tool `datastore_search_tool` with parameters: {'search_query': 'customer profile information'}"}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': 'For context:'}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': '[outstanding_balance_agent] `get-total-outstanding-balance` tool returned result: {\'result\': \'[{"SUM(outstanding_balance)":"22183.29"}]\'}'}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': 'For context:'}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': "[user_profile_agent] `datastore_search_tool` tool returned result: {'result': ['Example National Bank\\nSubject: Customer Profile of Chris Scott\\nDate: 15 Oct 2025\\n\\nChris has been a customer of the bank for 10 years now.\\nThey are married with two children.\\nThey are employed at Excaliburs Excellent Eatery as the executive chef.\\nIn that time, they have taken out eight loans, two of which are currently active.\\nTheir first loan was a personal loan that they had trouble paying on time, however they\\nhave since paid off that loan and paid all fees associated with their late payments. Since\\nthen, they are late on average of two or three times a year on their payments, although\\nnever later than 45 days late.\\nLoan Officer,\\nN. O. Medown', 'Example National Bank\\n\\nFrom: Pat Medown, Chief Loan Officer\\nTo: All loan officers\\n\\nDate: 1 Oct 2025\\n\\nEveryone,\\nIt has come to my attention recently that there has been some confusion about the criteria that\\nwe use in evaluating applicants for new loans. This has caused some inconsistencies with our\\nloan approvals, and have caused some significant negative feelings. To address this, I wanted\\nto clarify things, while still giving you loan officers the flexibility to evaluate specific cases as\\nneeded.\\n\\nThere are different criteria for both the debt:equity ratio and for the “personal evaluation” criteria\\nthat is in the customer’s file. In general, this criteria varies by loan type, however in some cases\\nit also varies by the amount requested. See below.\\nTo clarify some terms, since some of you seem to have forgotten:\\n● “Debt to Equity Ratio” determines the most debt they may have with us (including\\nexisting and new loans) based on how much they have on deposit with us. You can\\ndetermine the minimum equity by taking the total debt and dividing it by this number.\\n● “Customer rating” is a simplified rating that is based on the latest summary written by a\\nloan officer that is in the client’s file. This will evaluate their entire loan history with the\\nbank, not just current loans. While the officer does not need to specify this rating\\nprecisely, you can keep these descriptions in mind, going from best to worst:\\n○ Excellent: The client has a perfect record with us, with several loans whose\\npayments were never late. This should be reserved only for our best clients.\\n○ Great: The client has had several loans with us with only infrequent late\\npayments.\\n○ Good: The client either has only a few loans with us that have never been late, or\\nseveral loans with occasional late payments.\\n○ Fair: The client has a few loans with us that are sometimes late.\\n○ Poor: The client has no loans with us or loans that are frequently late.']}"}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': 'For context:'}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': '[outstanding_balance_agent] called tool `get-total-outstanding-balance` with parameters: {}'}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': 'For context:'}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': "[user_profile_agent] called tool `datastore_search_tool` with parameters: {'search_query': 'customer profile information'}"}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': 'For context:'}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': '[outstanding_balance_agent] `get-total-outstanding-balance` tool returned result: {\'result\': \'[{"SUM(outstanding_balance)":"22183.29"}]\'}'}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': 'For context:'}, {'kind': 'text', 'text': "[user_profile_agent] `datastore_search_tool` tool returned result: {'result': ['Example National Bank\\nSubject: Customer Profile of Chris Scott\\nDate: 15 Oct 2025\\n\\nChris has been a customer of the bank for 10 years now.\\nThey are married with two children.\\nThey are employed at Excaliburs Excellent Eatery as the executive chef.\\nIn that time, they have taken out eight loans, two of which are currently active.\\nTheir first loan was a personal loan that they had trouble paying on time, however they\\nhave since paid off that loan and paid all fees associated with their late payments.
